cc: @nalimilan @mschauer @ararslan @kleinschmidt @mbauman @andyferris @bramtayl @arnavs ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/32310/reactions","total_count":44,"+1":25,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":19,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/32310/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1124887234","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/32310#issuecomment-1124887234","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/32310","id":1124887234,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5DDGrC","user":{"login":"oschulz","id":546147,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU0NjE0Nw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/546147?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz","html_url":"https://github.com/oschulz","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-05-12T11:39:52Z","updated_at":"2022-05-12T11:39:52Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Here's an example statistics use case for the proposal above (merge PR as-is, add `AbstractSlices` to JuliaLang/Compat.jl#663 and establish a package like AbstractArrayOfArray to explore additional interfaces/functionality for sliced arrays like `getslicemap`):

Given

```julia
using LinearAlgebra, Distributions
Σs = rand(5,5)
d = MvNormal(randn(5), Σs * Σs')
xs_c = eachcol(rand(d, 100))
xs_r = eachrow(rand(d, 100)')
```

we currently need to `collect`, because `loglikelihood` currently only supports `AbstractArray{<:AbstractArray{<:Real, N}}`:

```julia
loglikelihood(d, collect(xs_c))
loglikelihood(d, collect(xs_r))
```

Now we'd be able to define

```
using AbstractArraysOfArrays: SlicedArrayLike, getslicemap

function Distributions.loglikelihood(
    d::Distribution{ArrayLikeVariate{N}}, xs::SlicedArrayLike,
) where {N}
    ll_impl(d, parent(xs), getslicemap(xs))
end

function ll_impl(d::Distribution{ArrayLikeVariate{N}}, X::AbstractArray{<:Real,M}, ::Tuple{Colon,Int64}) where {N,M}
    # Currently not super-fast because the generic `loglikelihood` implementation
    # operates col-by-col, but could be specialized for MvNormal to use `d.Σ.chol.L \ X`
    # and the like:
    loglikelihood(d, X)
end

function ll_impl(d::Distribution{ArrayLikeVariate{N}}, X::AbstractArray{<:Real,M}, ::Tuple{Int64,Colon}) where {N,M}
    # Could be specialized for MvNormal, see above:
    loglikelihood(d, X')
end
```

This could would work without `collect` now, and could become very efficient (and even GPU-compatible), from
Julia v1.6 upwards:

```julia
loglikelihood(d, xs_c)
loglikelihood(d, xs_r)
```

`eachrow`, `eachcol` (introduced in #29749) now return an `SliceArray` object (along with `Rows`/`Columns` aliases). The main benefit is that it will allow dispatch on the iterator to provide more efficient methods, e.g.
```
sum(A::Rows) = vec(sum(parent(A), dims=1))
```

This will encourage the use of `eachcol`/`eachrow` to resolve ambiguities in user-facing APIs, in particular, the "observations as rows vs columns" problem in the statistics/ML packages.

This also makes `eachslice` work over multiple dimensions.

Custom types can either have this field, or implement it using getproperty.

Isn't that a bit "hacky"? It would also be a cumbersome for sliced-array types to implement and would make it impossible to take advantage of dispatch. Also, refitting this to the legacy `eachrow`, `eachcol` and `eachslice` seems ugly.

I think some additional (not too much) interfaces will be required in `AbstractSlices` as well - in particular something that goes beyond `parent`, like a `flat_A, re_f = flatten_with_reconstruct(A)` which will return both the flattened array and a function to reconstruct a similar one. This will be important to have so code can efficiently implement broadcasted operations (e.g. transforming a vector-of-sample-vectors to a new vector-of-sample-vectors, both backed by flat arrays under the hood) without depending on various nested-array-implementations.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1128670559/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Sure, it it becomes lightweight enough (for packages like StatsBase - `var` and `cov` could certainly profit - to take it on as a dependency). I was planning to rename AbstractArraysOfArrays.jl to SlicedArrayInterface.jl anyway, to stress that it's not specific to ArraysOfArrays.jl - adding it to a lightweight ArrayInterfacesCore.jl instead would make sense.

@ChrisRackauckas, @Tokazama, would there be space for a lightweight interface for sliced arrays in ArrayInterfacesCore.jl,
like `getslicemap` and `flatten_and_reconstruct`? I would be very tiny, load-time wise.

> My point is to avoid scattering interfaces over many small packages.

I guess we should strive for a balance - not too many, but also not too few and too heavy ones. With InverseFunctions.jl and ChangesOfVariables.jl for example, me and @devmotion decided to go for two separate interface packages in the end - looking back I think thats was the right decision. But in this case, it also agree would fit very well into the scope of ArrayInterfacesCore.jl.

Proposal: I implement the interface in an unregistered SlicedArrayInterface.jl (should have this done in a few days), with a demo use case for StatsBase, Distributions and Bijectors. I think I can make a convincing demo for each of them. I'll invite everyone here and the ArrayInterface maintainers (@ChrisRackauckas, @Tokazama ?) for comments on that sliced array interface. And we'll ask the maintainers of packages like the ones mentioned if ArrayInterfaceCore.jl would be an acceptable dependency.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1132840626/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

`eachrow`, `eachcol` (introduced in #29749) now return an `SliceArray` object (along with `Rows`/`Columns` aliases). The main benefit is that it will allow dispatch on the iterator to provide more efficient methods, e.g.
```
sum(A::Rows) = vec(sum(parent(A), dims=1))
```

This will encourage the use of `eachcol`/`eachrow` to resolve ambiguities in user-facing APIs, in particular, the "observations as rows vs columns" problem in the statistics/ML packages.

This also makes `eachslice` work over multiple dimensions.

TODOs:
- [x] Figure out how to handle `eltype`: I'd appreciate some help on how best to do this.

`eachrow`, `eachcol` (introduced in #29749) now return an `SliceArray` object (along with `Rows`/`Columns` aliases). The main benefit is that it will allow dispatch on the iterator to provide more efficient methods, e.g.
```
sum(A::Rows) = vec(sum(parent(A), dims=1))
```

This will encourage the use of `eachcol`/`eachrow` to resolve ambiguities in user-facing APIs, in particular, the "observations as rows vs columns" problem in the statistics/ML packages.

This also makes `eachslice` work over multiple dimensions.

TODOs:
- [x] Figure out how to handle `eltype`: I'd appreciate some help on how best to do this.

`eachrow`, `eachcol` (introduced in #29749) now return an `SliceArray` object (along with `Rows`/`Columns` aliases). The main benefit is that it will allow dispatch on the iterator to provide more efficient methods, e.g.
```
sum(A::Rows) = vec(sum(parent(A), dims=1))
```

This will encourage the use of `eachcol`/`eachrow` to resolve ambiguities in user-facing APIs, in particular, the "observations as rows vs columns" problem in the statistics/ML packages.

This also makes `eachslice` work over multiple dimensions.

TODOs:
- [x] Figure out how to handle `eltype`: I'd appreciate some help on how best to do this.

I can see how that would be easy to fall into.

Yes, exactly. I need lot's of genric code that handles samples, and they may all have weight 1 - in which case important optimizations can be made or they may have arbitrary integer of floating point weights. So it would seem natural to use something like `Ones{StaticInt}(n)` (n can be very large) or `Fill(static(1), n`. But even with certain possible specializations (see JuliaArrays/FillArrays.jl#177) one can still end up with an `AbstractArray{StaticInt}` after a while. So it would be important that standard operations like `sum`, `mapreduce` etc. function efficiently on arrays of static numbers, and ideally propagate the "staticness" of the element type.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Static.jl/issues/comments/1090191758/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Hm

```julia
A = fill(static(1), 10)
sum(A)
```

comes down to

```
mapreduce(identity, Base.add_sum, A)
```

I think the culprit may be

```
julia> x = static(1)
static(1)

julia> x + x
static(2)

julia> Base.add_sum(x, x)
2
```

This seems to be due to the return-type assertion in the definition of `Base.add_sum`

```julia
add_sum(x::Real, y::Real)::Real = x + y
```

Since

```julia
julia> foo(a::Real, b::Real) = a + b
foo (generic function with 1 method)

julia> foo(x, x)
static(2)

julia> bar(a::Real, b::Real)::Real = a + b
bar (generic function with 1 method)

julia> bar(x, x)
2
```

which I don't get, since `static(2) isa Real`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Static.jl/issues/comments/1090201958/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```julia
julia> A = fill(static(4.2), 10^5);

julia> @btime mapreduce(sqrt, Base.add_sum, A)
  16.529 ms (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

Could we add a specialization like

```julia
function Base.mapreduce(f, ::typeof(Base.add_sum), A::AbstractVector{T}) where {T<:StaticFloat64}
    length(eachindex(A)) * T()
end
```

it would get us

```julia
julia> @btime mapreduce(sqrt, Base.add_sum, A)
  73.947 ns (1 allocation: 16 bytes)
420000.0
```

I don't really have an MWE, and the behavior is intermittent. I've used Revise very extensively with the package on Julia v1.7 without any trouble.

Steps to reproduce (starting with a completly empty `DEPOT_PATH`), all steps in a single Julia session (segfault doesn't always occur):

```
julia> versioninfo()

julia> DEPOT_PATH

julia> bat_sample(BAT.example_posterior());

ijl_apply_generic at /buildworker/worker/package_linux64/build/src/gf.c:2536
```

This runs fine on Julia v1.7.2 (segfault never occurs).

More compact version of steps-to-reproduce, without switching REPL modes:

```
using Pkg
pkg"add Revise@3.3.3"
using Revise

pkg"activate --temp"
pkg"dev BAT"
cd(dirname(dirname(Base.find_package("BAT"))))

run(`git checkout revise-test-1`)

pkg"precompile"
using BAT
bat_sample(BAT.example_posterior());

run(`git checkout revise-test-2`)

bat_sample(BAT.example_posterior());
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44954/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44954/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1097656432","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44954#issuecomment-1097656432","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44954","id":1097656432,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5BbOhw","user":{"login":"oschulz","id":546147,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU0NjE0Nw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/546147?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz","html_url":"https://github.com/oschulz","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-04-13T07:33:17Z","updated_at":"2022-04-13T07:33:17Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Here's a new version of the test script using two different BAT branches, this one seems to be more reproducible:

Steps to reproduce (starting with a completly empty `DEPOT_PATH`), all steps in a single Julia session (segfault doesn't always occur):

```

This runs fine on Julia v1.7.2 (segfault never occurs).

More compact version of steps-to-reproduce, without switching REPL modes:

```
using Pkg
pkg"add Revise@3.3.3"
using Revise

pkg"activate --temp"
pkg"dev BAT"
cd(dirname(dirname(Base.find_package("BAT"))))

run(`git checkout revise-test-1`)

pkg"precompile"
using BAT
bat_sample(BAT.example_posterior());

run(`git checkout revise-test-2`)

bat_sample(BAT.example_posterior());
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44954/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44954/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1097713105","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44954#issuecomment-1097713105","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44954","id":1097713105,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5BbcXR","user":{"login":"oschulz","id":546147,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU0NjE0Nw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/546147?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz","html_url":"https://github.com/oschulz","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oschulz/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-04-13T08:35:14Z","updated_at":"2022-04-13T08:35:14Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Running the test script above in one go makes the disk explode, but running it in two stages works.

docstring"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":36,"created_at":"2020-05-11T00:27:41Z","updated_at":"2022-04-27T08:46:41Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/35833","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/35833","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/35833.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/35833.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR proposes *context variables* API that can be used to propagate context-dependent information across task boundaries.  It is conceptually similar to `task_local_storage` but with a main difference that it "copies" all key-value pairs to child `Task`s.  The context variables are conceptually similar to dynamically scoped variables.

## Motivations

There are several places context variables can be useful or required.

### `ENV`

It was proposed to use `task_local_storage` to fix thread-safety of `withenv` https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34726#issuecomment-586138687 by maintaining task-local copy (or overlay) of `ENV`.  However, it would mean that the environment variables cannot cross task boundaries.  Context variable can fix this shortcoming.

### `@testset`

`@testset` uses `task_local_storage` to track current active test set.  However, following example does not work (prints `No tests`) because the information of the current test does not propagate to the child task:

```julia
@testset begin
    @sync @async @test true
end
```

### Logging

`Task` has the `logstate` field that propagates to child tasks; i.e., it works as a hard-coded context variable.  Once context variable handling is sufficiently matured, it may be possible to eliminate the special handling from `Task` and use a context variable for `Task`.  Furthermore, context variable allows users to develop logging-like interfaces.

### Custom worker pool abstraction

In #35757 and [Propagation of available/assigned worker-IDs in hierarchical computations? - Domains / Julia at Scale - JuliaLang](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/propagation-of-available-assigned-worker-ids-in-hierarchical-computations/38670), it was brought up that propagating "computation resources" (thread/process pools, etc.) across tasks and processes is required for implementing custom worker pool interfaces.

### Misc.

> Request-related data, such as security tokens and request data in web applications, language context for gettext, etc.
>
> --- [Rationale section in PEP 0567](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/#rationale)

It would be useful for implementing a better nestable progress information handling https://github.com/JunoLab/ProgressLogging.jl/pull/13#discussion_r371057967.

## Other languages

### Implicit context

* Python has [`contextvars`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/contextvars.html). See [PEP 567](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/) and [PEP 550](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/) for discussion.
* .NET has [`ExecutionContext`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.threading.executioncontext?view=netcore-3.1).
* Kotlin has [`CoroutineContext`](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/reference/coroutines/coroutine-context-and-dispatchers.html).

### Explicit context

* Go has [`context`](https://blog.golang.org/context).

## Proposed design

I propose an API with the following basic usages.

* Declare context variable: `@contextvar x`
* Get the value: `x[]`
* Set the value: `x[] = value`

For a tutorial and the full reference API of the proposed design, see https://tkf.github.io/ContextVariablesX.jl/dev/

Internally, `@contextvar` creates an instance of `ContextVar` which is defined as

```julia
struct ContextVar{T}
    key::UUID
    default::T
    ...
end
```

Then `x[]` and `x[] = value` invoke `current_task().ctxvars[x.key]` and an "immutable version" of `current_task().ctxvars[x.key] = value`, respectively (roughly speaking).

This has a couple of nice properties:

1. `x[]` can be inferred
2. `x` is forced to be namespaced (i.e., it has to exist in some module name space.)
3. `x` can be backed up by an efficient concrete key type (e.g., UUID)
4. `x` allows small-size optimization when the value type can be inlined into the context storage (in principle)

See also https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35757 which already contains some discussion on this API.

I don't think it's only useful for concurrency, it's about propagating argument-like "settings" that can't be well handled via function arguments. Things like logger, progress monitor, choice of computational resources, etc. This is of course very important for thread- and process-level parallelism, but I think it would benefit serial chains of function calls as well.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1110730509/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

are some very fast mallocs out there:

http://www.canonware.com/jemalloc/
http://goog-perftools.sourceforge.net/doc/tcmalloc.html

It would be useful to try out these instead of the system malloc, to see if we get a real performance boost. I do feel that using jemalloc, rather than the system one, may also make performance and memory behaviour more uniform across different OSes.

I see use of malloc in src/, src/flisp, and src/support. It would also be nice to refactor the code so that other malloc implementations can be experimented with easily.

I guess the libraries in external will continue using their own malloc.

* .NET has [`ExecutionContext`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.threading.executioncontext?view=netcore-3.1).
* Kotlin has [`CoroutineContext`](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/reference/coroutines/coroutine-context-and-dispatchers.html).

### Explicit context

* Go has [`context`](https://blog.golang.org/context).

## Proposed design

I propose an API with the following basic usages.

* Declare context variable: `@contextvar x`
* Get the value: `x[]`
* Set the value: `x[] = value`

For a tutorial and the full reference API of the proposed design, see https://tkf.github.io/ContextVariablesX.jl/dev/

Internally, `@contextvar` creates an instance of `ContextVar` which is defined as

```julia
struct ContextVar{T}
    key::UUID
    default::T
    ...
end
```

Then `x[]` and `x[] = value` invoke `current_task().ctxvars[x.key]` and an "immutable version" of `current_task().ctxvars[x.key] = value`, respectively (roughly speaking).

This has a couple of nice properties:

1. `x[]` can be inferred
2. `x` is forced to be namespaced (i.e., it has to exist in some module name space.)
3. `x` can be backed up by an efficient concrete key type (e.g., UUID)
4. `x` allows small-size optimization when the value type can be inlined into the context storage (in principle)

See also https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35757 which already contains some discussion on this API.

PR proposes *context variables* API that can be used to propagate context-dependent information across task boundaries.  It is conceptually similar to `task_local_storage` but with a main difference that it "copies" all key-value pairs to child `Task`s.  The context variables are conceptually similar to dynamically scoped variables.

## Motivations

There are several places context variables can be useful or required.

### `ENV`

It was proposed to use `task_local_storage` to fix thread-safety of `withenv` https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34726#issuecomment-586138687 by maintaining task-local copy (or overlay) of `ENV`.  However, it would mean that the environment variables cannot cross task boundaries.  Context variable can fix this shortcoming.

### `@testset`

`@testset` uses `task_local_storage` to track current active test set.  However, following example does not work (prints `No tests`) because the information of the current test does not propagate to the child task:

```julia
@testset begin
    @sync @async @test true
end
```

### Logging

`Task` has the `logstate` field that propagates to child tasks; i.e., it works as a hard-coded context variable.  Once context variable handling is sufficiently matured, it may be possible to eliminate the special handling from `Task` and use a context variable for `Task`.  Furthermore, context variable allows users to develop logging-like interfaces.

### Custom worker pool abstraction

In #35757 and [Propagation of available/assigned worker-IDs in hierarchical computations? - Domains / Julia at Scale - JuliaLang](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/propagation-of-available-assigned-worker-ids-in-hierarchical-computations/38670), it was brought up that propagating "computation resources" (thread/process pools, etc.) across tasks and processes is required for implementing custom worker pool interfaces.

### Misc.

> Request-related data, such as security tokens and request data in web applications, language context for gettext, etc.
>
> --- [Rationale section in PEP 0567](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/#rationale)

It would be useful for implementing a better nestable progress information handling https://github.com/JunoLab/ProgressLogging.jl/pull/13#discussion_r371057967.

## Other languages

### Implicit context

* Python has [`contextvars`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/contextvars.html). See [PEP 567](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/) and [PEP 550](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/) for discussion.
* .NET has [`ExecutionContext`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.threading.executioncontext?view=netcore-3.1).
* Kotlin has [`CoroutineContext`](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/reference/coroutines/coroutine-context-and-dispatchers.html).

### Explicit context

* Go has [`context`](https://blog.golang.org/context).

## Proposed design

I propose an API with the following basic usages.

* Declare context variable: `@contextvar x`
* Get the value: `x[]`
* Set the value: `x[] = value`

For a tutorial and the full reference API of the proposed design, see https://tkf.github.io/ContextVariablesX.jl/dev/

Internally, `@contextvar` creates an instance of `ContextVar` which is defined as

```julia
struct ContextVar{T}
    key::UUID
    default::T
    ...
end
```

Then `x[]` and `x[] = value` invoke `current_task().ctxvars[x.key]` and an "immutable version" of `current_task().ctxvars[x.key] = value`, respectively (roughly speaking).

This has a couple of nice properties:

1. `x[]` can be inferred
2. `x` is forced to be namespaced (i.e., it has to exist in some module name space.)
3. `x` can be backed up by an efficient concrete key type (e.g., UUID)
4. `x` allows small-size optimization when the value type can be inlined into the context storage (in principle)

See also https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35757 which already contains some discussion on this API.
